name: Jaimini
dates_notes: Jaimini is named in the Brahma-Sūtra as a Vedāntic ācārya and in the Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra as its compiler-author; the relative position of the two sūtra-corpora and their cross-references makes the dating mutually constraining. Frauwallner's Geschichte der indischen Philosophie places Jaimini in the third to second century BCE (Frauwallner not on disk in our corpus; cited via field-standard summary). The biographical figure is a name attached to the founding sūtra-text.

lineage_polemical.note: The Brahma-Sūtra's first substantive sūtra after the maṅgala — 1.1.4, tat tu samanvayāt — is read by every later Vedāntic bhāṣyakāra as Bādarāyaṇa's response to the Mīmāṃsā prioritization of the karma-kāṇḍa: śruti's tātparya converges on brahman, not merely on enjoined ritual action. The Brahma-Sūtra cites Jaimini by name on several disputed adhikaraṇas (e.g., 1.2.31, 3.4.2, 4.4.5), the relation is constructive on śruti's pramāṇa-status and polemical on the supremacy of dharma-jijñāsā versus brahma-jijñāsā.

core_thesis: Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* is the founding text of *Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā*, the *darśana* of Vedic ritual exegesis. The opening *sūtra* — *athāto dharma-jijñāsā* (Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.1), "now, therefore, the inquiry into *dharma*" — fixes the discipline's object: not the cosmos as such, but *dharma*, defined at MS 1.1.2 as *codanā-lakṣaṇo'rtho dharmaḥ*, "*dharma* is the *artha* characterized by Vedic injunction." The decisive epistemological thesis is given at MS 1.1.5: śabda — specifically, the unauthored (*apauruṣeya*), eternal Vedic *codanā* — is an autonomous and self-validating *pramāṇa* for *dharma*; no extra-Vedic warrant for ritual action is required or available. From this follow several distinctive Mīmāṃsā doctrines that Vedānta inherits or contests: (i) the eternity and non-authorship of the Veda (MS 1.1.5, *apauruṣeyatvādhikaraṇa*); (ii) the *śabda-nityatva* doctrine — that linguistic units (*varṇa*, *pada*, *vākya*) are themselves eternal universals (MS 1.1.6–23, *autpattika-sūtra* and following); (iii) *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* — the intrinsic validity of cognition pending positive defeat (MS 1.1.2, on Śabara's reading); (iv) the analysis of every Vedic sentence as either *vidhi* (injunction), *arthavāda* (commendation), *mantra*, or *nāmadheya* (MS 1.4.1–9); (v) the rejection of *īśvara* as required for the *karma-phala-saṃbandha*, the *apūrva* doctrine substituting an unseen-result mechanism for divine bookkeeping (MS 2.1.5, *apūrvādhikaraṇa*).

engaged_works.title: Mimamsa-Sutra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by mutual citation with the Brahma-Sūtra and by the relative position to the early Dharma-Sūtras.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Uniformly ascribed to Jaimini by tradition; the surviving twelve-adhyāya text is likely the consolidation of an earlier oral sūtra-tradition, paralleling the Brahma-Sūtra's compositional history.

ascription_evidence.description: Frauwallner Geschichte der indischen Philosophie I and Clooney 1990 Thinking Ritually are the standard scholarly references for the ascription; neither work is on disk in our corpus, cited via field-standard summary.

engaged_works.summary: Twelve *adhyāyas*, each in four *pādas*, of about 2745 *sūtras* — the largest of the *darśanika* sūtra-corpora. Clooney's *Thinking Ritually* (1990) is the standard English-language secondary reading of the work as a coherent ritual-cum-reflective system (the work is not on disk in our corpus; cited via title-thesis and known scholarly summary). The first *adhyāya* establishes the foundational doctrines: *codanā* as the autonomous *pramāṇa* for *dharma*, the eternity of *śabda*, the four sentence-types (*vidhi*, *arthavāda*, *mantra*, *nāmadheya*), and the *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* of cognition. *Adhyāyas* 2–6 develop the analytical apparatus for ritual interpretation — the *sad-vidha-tātparya-liṅga* (the six marks of intended meaning), the *aṅga-aṅgi-vibhāga* (analysis of subordinate and principal ritual elements), and the procedure-rules (*prakaraṇa*, *sthāna*, *krama*, *samākhyā*). *Adhyāyas* 7–9 treat *atideśa* — the extension of one ritual's procedure to another — and *bādha*. *Adhyāya* 10 treats *ūha* (modification) and *bādha* (cancellation). *Adhyāya* 11 treats *tantra* (the unification of multiple ritual elements). *Adhyāya* 12 treats *prasaṅga* (the contingent extension of one ritual element to another). The work cites by name the ācāryas Bādari, Bādarāyaṇa, Lābukāyana, and others — direct evidence of an earlier oral *sūtra*-tradition. The text is unintelligible without commentary; the principal commentary is Śabara's *Śābara-Bhāṣya*.
